examples.explanation: Initialize empty trie. Insert 'cat': root.children[2] (c) created, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] (a) created, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] (t) created, node=node_t; mark node_t.is_end=true. Search 'cat': root.children[2] exists, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] exists, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] exists, node=node_t; node_t.is_end=true, return true.

Initialize empty trie. Insert 'car': root.children[2] (c) created, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] (a) created, node=node_a; node_a.children[17] (r) created, node=node_r; mark node_r.is_end=true. Search 'cat': root.children[2] exists, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] exists, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] (t) does not exist (null), return false.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Standard Trie Representation
approaches.summary: Use a standard trie where each node stores an array or hash map of children pointers for all possible characters, leading to high memory usage due to many null pointers. This is the baseline approach before any compression.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) for insertion and search, where L is the length of the key
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N * A) worst-case, where N is the number of nodes and A is the alphabet size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Standard Trie with Insertion and Search Operations
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a standard trie data structure supporting insertion and search operations.

examples.trace: Initialize empty trie. Insert 'cat': root.children[2] (c) created, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] (a) created, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] (t) created, node=node_t; mark node_t.is_end=true. Search 'cat': root.children[2] exists, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] exists, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] exists, node=node_t; node_t.is_end=true, return true.

Initialize empty trie. Insert 'car': root.children[2] (c) created, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] (a) created, node=node_a; node_a.children[17] (r) created, node=node_r; mark node_r.is_end=true. Search 'cat': root.children[2] exists, node=node_c; node_c.children[0] exists, node=node_a; node_a.children[19] (t) does not exist (null), return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trie Insertion Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the insertion operation for adding a word to a standard trie.
ladders.explanation:
The insertion operation is crucial as it builds the trie structure. The intuition is to traverse the trie following characters of the word, creating new nodes only when necessary, thus sharing prefixes. This ensures efficient storage and O(L) time.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start at root node.
2. For each character in the word:
   a. Calculate index (e.g., 0-25 for 'a'-'z').
   b. If child at index is null, create new TrieNode.
   c. Move current node to this child.
3. After processing all characters, mark current node as end of word.

Time complexity: O(L) where L is word length. Space complexity: O(L) in worst-case if all nodes are new.

Connection to level above: This is the core operation that populates the trie for search functionality.

Common mistakes: Not creating nodes for all characters, forgetting to mark end, incorrect character-to-index conversion.

Real code pattern:
def insert(self, word):
    node = self.root
    for ch in word:
        idx = ord(ch) - ord('a')
        if not node.children[idx]:
            node.children[idx] = TrieNode()
        node = node.children[idx]
    node.is_end = True

examples.output: Trie with nodes for d, o, g
examples.trace: Start at root. Character 'd': idx=3, root.children[3] is null, create node_d, move to node_d. Character 'o': idx=14, node_d.children[14] is null, create node_o, move to node_o. Character 'g': idx=6, node_o.children[6] is null, create node_g, move to node_g. Mark node_g.is_end=true.

Trie has root->node_d->node_o->node_g from previous. Start at root. Character 'd': idx=3, root.children[3] exists (node_d), move to node_d. Character 'o': idx=14, node_d.children[14] exists (node_o), move to node_o. Mark node_o.is_end=true (now both 'do' and 'dog' are stored).

testCases.expected: No nodes created, root.is_end remains false

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character-to-Index Mapping and Node Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to map characters to array indices and traverse nodes in a trie.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central technique for efficient trie operations. The intuition is that by mapping each character to a fixed index (e.g., 0-25 for lowercase letters), we can use array indexing for O(1) access to child nodes. Traversal involves moving from parent to child nodes based on characters, enabling prefix matching.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. For a given character, compute index = ord(character) - ord('a') for lowercase English.
2. To traverse: start at current node, use index to access children array.
3. If children[index] is not null, move to that node; otherwise, character is not present.

Time complexity: O(1) per character for index calculation and access. Space complexity: O(1) extra.

Connection to level above: This logic is used in both insertion and search operations.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in index calculation, not handling non-lowercase characters, assuming array is initialized.

Real code pattern:
def get_index(ch):
    return ord(ch) - ord('a')

def traverse(node, ch):
    idx = get_index(ch)
    if node.children[idx]:
        return node.children[idx]
    return None

examples.input: Map 'c' to index and traverse from root
examples.trace: Character 'c': ord('c')=99, ord('a')=97, index=99-97=2. From root node, access root.children[2]. If not null, return that node; else return null.
Character 'z': ord('z')=122, ord('a')=97, index=122-97=25. From current node, access node.children[25]. If exists, move to it; else stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure with Children Array
ladders.desc: Define the TrieNode class with an array for children pointers and end-of-word flag.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept establishes the fundamental unit of a trie. The intuition is that each node must store potential children for all alphabet characters to enable fast traversal, and a flag to indicate complete words. Using an array provides O(1) access but wastes memory with null pointers.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create class TrieNode.
2. Initialize children as array of size 26 (for 'a'-'z') with null values.
3. Initialize is_end as false.

Time complexity: O(1) for node creation. Space complexity: O(A) per node where A is alphabet size.

Connection to level above: This structure enables the character mapping and traversal logic.

Common mistakes: Using wrong array size, not initializing all elements to null, forgetting is_end flag.

Real code pattern:
class TrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = [None] * 26
        self.is_end = False

examples.trace: Initialize new object. Set children as list of 26 None values. Set is_end to False. Memory allocated for 26 pointers.

examples.input: Create node and set is_end true
examples.trace: Create TrieNode as above. Set is_end = True. Children array remains all null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Initialization and Access
ladders.desc: Initialize an array of fixed size and access elements by index.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation is essential for implementing the children array in a TrieNode. The intuition is that arrays provide contiguous memory storage with O(1) access time using indices. Initializing with null values sets up empty slots for future node pointers.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Declare array of size N (e.g., 26).
2. Initialize all elements to null (or None in Python).
3. Access element at index i using array[i].
4. Modify element by assigning to array[i].

Time complexity: O(N) for initialization, O(1) for access. Space complexity: O(N).

Connection to level above: This operation creates the children storage in TrieNode.

Common mistakes: Index out of bounds, not initializing all elements, confusing index with value.

Real code pattern:
# Initialize
children = [None] * 26
# Access
child = children[5]
# Modify
children[5] = new_node

examples.trace: Create list with 5 elements, all set to None. Memory allocated for 5 pointers.
Start with array [null, null, null, null, null]. Access children[2] (null). Set children[2] = 10. Now array is [null, null, 10, null, null].

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Characters and ASCII Values
ladders.desc: Understand characters as data types and their ASCII integer representations.
ladders.explanation:
This is the simplest prerequisite for trie implementation. The intuition is that computers store characters as numbers (ASCII codes), allowing arithmetic operations. For example, lowercase 'a' to 'z' have consecutive codes 97-122, enabling index calculation by subtraction.

Step-by-step concept:
1. Characters like 'a', 'b' are symbols but stored as integers.
2. ASCII: 'a'=97, 'b'=98, ..., 'z'=122.
3. ord() function gets integer code of character.
4. Subtract ord('a') to map 'a' to 0, 'b' to 1, etc.

Time complexity: O(1) for ord() and subtraction. Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to level above: This enables character-to-index mapping for array access.

Common mistakes: Assuming all characters have same code, not handling case sensitivity, incorrect subtraction.

Real code pattern:
ch = 'c'
code = ord(ch)  # 99
index = code - ord('a')  # 99-97=2

examples.trace: Character 'a': ord('a') returns integer 97. Character 'b': ord('b') returns 98. They are consecutive.
Character 'd': ord('d')=100. ord('a')=97. Index = 100 - 97 = 3. This corresponds to position in alphabet.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Radix Trie (Patricia Trie)
approaches.summary: Compress the trie by merging nodes with single children into a single node that stores a string label, reducing the number of nodes and memory overhead. This is a common compression method that eliminates chains of single-child nodes.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) for operations, similar to standard trie but with fewer nodes
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N) in practice, where N is the number of nodes after compression, typically less than standard trie

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Radix Trie with Insert, Search, and Delete Operations
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a compressed trie (Radix/Patricia Trie) with all core operations.
ladders.explanation: The Radix Trie solves the memory inefficiency of standard tries by compressing chains of single-child nodes into single nodes with string labels. This reduces node count while maintaining O(L) time complexity for operations where L is the length of the key. Intuition: Instead of storing one character per node, we store substrings along edges, merging nodes where there's only one child path. This compression happens dynamically during insertions and deletions. Step-by-step: 1) Define a TrieNode class with children dictionary and is_end flag. 2) Insert: Traverse matching prefixes, split nodes when mismatches occur, create new nodes for remaining parts. 3) Search: Traverse by matching prefixes in edge labels, return is_end if exact match found. 4) Delete: Remove nodes carefully, potentially merging nodes back if they become single-child after deletion. Time: O(L) per operation. Space: O(N) where N is compressed nodes. Connection to L1: This integrates the key insertion subroutine with search/delete. Common mistakes: Not handling edge splits correctly during insertion, forgetting to update is_end flags, improper node merging during deletion. Real pattern: Use recursive or iterative traversal with careful string matching.

examples.output: Search 'app' returns True, after deletion structure compresses
examples.trace: 1) Insert 'apple': Create root->'apple' node with is_end=True. 2) Insert 'app': Traverse 'apple' edge, split at length 3: root->'app' node (is_end=True) with child 'le' node (is_end=True from 'apple'). 3) Insert 'banana': root->'banana' node. 4) Search 'app': Match 'app' edge exactly, check is_end=True → True. 5) Delete 'apple': Remove 'le' node, 'app' node remains with is_end=True, no merge needed as 'app' has no other children.

examples.output: Search 'car' returns True, after deletion 'cat' and 'card' remain compressed
examples.trace: 1) Insert 'cat': root->'cat' node. 2) Insert 'car': Traverse 'cat' edge, split at 'ca': root->'ca' node with children 't' and 'r'. 3) Insert 'card': Traverse 'car' path, split 'r' into 'r' (is_end=True for 'car') with child 'd'. 4) Search 'car': Match 'ca' then 'r', is_end=True → True. 5) Delete 'car': Remove is_end from 'r' node, check merge: 'r' has one child 'd', so merge 'r' and 'd' into 'rd' node under 'ca'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Operation with Node Splitting and Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the insert function that adds a key to the Radix Trie while compressing nodes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial because insertion drives the compression mechanism. Intuition: When inserting a key, we traverse existing nodes matching prefixes. If a mismatch occurs, we split the current node's label at the mismatch point, creating a new branch. This automatically compresses single-child chains by storing substrings. Step-by-step: 1) Start at root, iterate through children. 2) Find child with matching prefix. 3) If full match, update is_end. 4) If partial match, split the edge: create a new internal node with the common prefix, attach the remaining parts as children. 5) If no match, create a new node. Time: O(L) for key length L. Space: O(1) extra per insertion. Connection to L0: This is the core of building the trie; combined with search/delete for full solution. Common mistakes: Incorrect split index calculation, not updating is_end flags properly, forgetting to handle empty key case. Real pattern: Use while loop to traverse, compare strings character by character for splits.

examples.input: Insert 'apple' into empty trie, then insert 'app'
examples.output: Trie compresses to have 'app' node with child 'le'
examples.trace: 1) Insert 'apple': Root has no children, create new node with label 'apple', is_end=True. 2) Insert 'app': Traverse from root, find child 'apple' matches first 3 chars 'app'. Split: Create new node 'app' (is_end=True for 'app'), with child 'le' (is_end=True for 'apple'). Root now points to 'app' node.

examples.output: Trie has 'ca' node with children 't' and 'r'
examples.trace: 1) Insert 'cat': Root->'cat' node. 2) Insert 'car': Traverse 'cat', match 'ca'. Split: Create node 'ca' with children: 't' (is_end=True for 'cat') and 'r' (is_end=True for 'car'). Root points to 'ca'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix Matching and Node Splitting Mechanism
ladders.desc: Understand how to find matching prefixes and split nodes during trie traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique enabling compression. Intuition: Instead of storing one character per node, we group sequences along paths. When inserting or searching, we compare the key with node labels to find the longest common prefix. If it's a partial match, we split the node to insert the new key, which merges single-child chains. Step-by-step: 1) For a node with label S and key K, find the longest common prefix length L. 2) If L == len(S), move to child. 3) If L < len(S), split S into prefix S[:L] and suffix S[L:]. Create a new node for the prefix, attach the suffix as a child, and handle the remaining key K[L:]. Time: O(min(L, M)) for key length L and node label length M. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the insert operation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in prefix length, not handling empty strings, incorrect child pointer updates after split. Real pattern: Use a loop to compare characters until mismatch or end.

examples.trace: Compare 'apple' and 'app': characters match at indices 0,1,2. L=3. Since L < len('apple')=5, split: new label 'app', child with label 'le'. Key 'app' fully matched.

examples.output: Split at L=2, prefix 'ca', suffix 't', remaining key 'r'
examples.trace: Compare 'cat' and 'car': match 'c','a', mismatch at index 2. L=2. Split 'cat' into 'ca' and 't'. Handle remaining key 'r' as new child under 'ca'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure with String Labels
ladders.desc: Design the TrieNode class that supports string labels instead of single characters.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because Radix Trie nodes store substrings, not just single characters. Intuition: Each node represents a segment of a key, allowing compression of multiple characters into one node. This reduces memory by decreasing the number of nodes. Step-by-step: 1) Define a class TrieNode with attributes: 'label' (string storing the substring), 'children' (dictionary mapping characters to child nodes), and 'is_end' (boolean indicating end of a key). 2) The label can be empty for root. 3) Children are indexed by the first character of their labels for efficient traversal. Time: O(1) for node access. Space: O(1) per node. Connection to L2: This structure enables the prefix matching and splitting logic. Common mistakes: Using single-character labels, not initializing children properly, forgetting is_end flag. Real pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self, label=''): self.label = label; self.children = {}; self.is_end = False.

examples.input: Create nodes for keys 'cat' and 'car' after compression
examples.trace: 1) Root node: label='', children={'c': node_ca}. 2) Node 'ca': label='ca', children={'t': node_t, 'r': node_r}, is_end=False. 3) Node 't': label='t', children={}, is_end=True. 4) Node 'r': label='r', children={}, is_end=True.
Root node: label='', children={'h': node_hello}. Node 'hello': label='hello', children={}, is_end=True.

testCases.input: Node for label 'test', is_end=True, no children
Node for label 'do', is_end=False, children 'g' and 't'
Node for label 'r', is_end=False, children 'un' and 'an'
Node for label 'dat', is_end=False, children 'a' and 'e'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Longest Common Prefix Between Two Strings
ladders.desc: Compute the length of the longest common prefix of two strings.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in Radix Trie for matching keys with node labels. Intuition: By comparing strings character by character from the start, we determine how much they share initially, which guides node splits and traversals. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize index i=0. 2) While i < min(len(str1), len(str2)) and str1[i] == str2[i], increment i. 3) Return i as the common prefix length. Time: O(min(L1, L2)) for string lengths L1, L2. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied to node labels and keys during insertion/search. Common mistakes: Not handling empty strings, off-by-one errors, using inefficient methods like slicing. Real pattern: Use a simple for loop or while loop with index comparison.

examples.trace: Compare chars: index 0 'a'=='a', index 1 'p'=='p', index 2 'p'=='p', index 3: str2 ends, stop. Common prefix length = 3.
Compare: index 0 'c'=='c', index 1 'a'=='a', index 2 't'!='r', stop. Length = 2.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Character Encoding for Tries
ladders.desc: Understand basic array operations and how to map characters to integers for use in trie structures.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for understanding tries. Intuition: A string is an ordered list of characters (e.g., 'cat' has characters 'c','a','t'). A prefix is a substring that starts at the beginning of the string (e.g., prefixes of 'apple' are '', 'a', 'ap', 'app', 'appl', 'apple'). In tries, we use prefixes to organize and search keys efficiently. Step-by-step: 1) Define a string as a sequence accessible by index. 2) A prefix of length k is the first k characters. 3) The empty string '' is a prefix of every string. Time: O(1) to access a character, O(k) to get a prefix of length k. Space: O(n) for string of length n. Connection to L4: This foundation is used to compute common prefixes. Common mistakes: Confusing prefixes with substrings, not considering empty prefix, off-by-one in length calculations. Real pattern: In Python, string slicing like s[:k] gets the prefix of length k.

examples.trace: Start with empty string: ''. Add first char: 'h'. Add second: 'he'. Third: 'hel'. Fourth: 'hell'. Fifth: 'hello'. Total 6 prefixes including empty.
Take first 2 characters: index 0 'c', index 1 'a', so prefix is 'ca'.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Double-Array Trie
approaches.summary: Implement a trie using two arrays (base and check) to represent transitions, achieving memory efficiency by compact storage and fast lookups. This method is optimal for static tries where keys do not change frequently.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) for operations, with constant-time transitions due to array indexing
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N + A) where N is the number of nodes and A is the alphabet size, more space-efficient than standard trie

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Double-Array Trie Compression
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a compressed trie using base and check arrays for memory-efficient storage and fast lookups.
ladders.explanation: The Double-Array Trie (DAT) compresses a standard trie into two integer arrays: base and check. Each node is represented by an index in these arrays. For a node s, base[s] gives the starting index for its children, and check[t] for a child index t confirms that t's parent is s. This achieves O(L) time for operations (insert, search, delete) with constant-time transitions via array indexing, and O(N + A) space where N is nodes and A is alphabet size, making it optimal for static tries. Intuition: Instead of storing pointers to children, we compute child indices using base values and verify parent-child relationships with check, allowing dense packing of nodes into arrays. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize base and check arrays with size M (e.g., 1000), set base[0]=1, check[0]=0 (root). 2. Insert key: For each character c in key, compute t = base[s] + char_code(c). If check[t] == s, move to t; else if check[t] == -1 (empty), set check[t]=s and base[t]=base[s] (initially), then move; else resolve collision by relocating nodes. 3. Search: Similar traversal, return true if end state is terminal (marked separately). 4. Delete: Mark node as unused, but in practice, DAT is static, so deletion is often omitted. Time: O(L) per operation; space: O(M) for arrays, with M ≈ N + A. Connection to L1: This uses the insertion subroutine to build the trie. Common mistakes: Not handling collisions properly, leading to infinite loops; use a relocation method like finding a new base for the conflicting node. Real code pattern: class DoubleArrayTrie: def __init__(self): self.base = [0]*M; self.check = [-1]*M; self.base[0]=1; self.check[0]=0; self.terminal = set().

examples.output: Trie built with base and check arrays
examples.trace: Initialize: base=[1,0,...], check=[0,-1,...]. Insert "cat": char c (code 2): t=base[0]+2=3, check[3]==-1, set check[3]=0, base[3]=base[0]=1, s=3. char a (code 0): t=base[3]+0=1, check[1]==-1, set check[1]=3, base[1]=base[3]=1, s=1. char t (code 19): t=base[1]+19=20, check[20]==-1, set check[20]=1, base[20]=base[1]=1, mark terminal at 20. Insert "car": from root, c to 3, a to 1, r (code 17): t=base[1]+17=18, check[18]==-1, set check[18]=1, base[18]=base[1]=1, mark terminal at 18. Insert "dog": char d (code 3): t=base[0]+3=4, check[4]==-1, set check[4]=0, base[4]=base[0]=1, s=4. char o (code 14): t=base[4]+14=18, check[18]==1 (occupied by r from car), collision! Relocate node 1: find new base for 1 such that all children (indices base[1]+char_code) are free. Suppose new base[1]=5, update base[1]=5, move children: for a at index 1 (old), new index=5+0=5, set check[5]=1, base[5]=base[old]=1; for r at 18, new index=5+17=22, set check[22]=1, base[22]=1, free old indices. Now for dog: from root, d to 4, o: t=base[4]+14=18, check[18]==-1 (after relocation), set check[18]=4, base[18]=base[4]=1, s=18. char g (code 6): t=base[18]+6=24, check[24]==-1, set check[24]=18, base[24]=base[18]=1, mark terminal at 24.

examples.input: Search keys: ["cat", "cap"] in trie from example 1
examples.trace: Trie from example 1 has nodes for cat, car, dog. Search "cat": start s=0, c: t=base[0]+2=3, check[3]==0, s=3. a: t=base[3]+0=1, check[1]==3, s=1. t: t=base[1]+19=20, check[20]==1, s=20, check if terminal: yes (marked), return True. Search "cap": start s=0, c: t=3, check[3]==0, s=3. a: t=1, check[1]==3, s=1. p (code 15): t=base[1]+15=16, check[16]==-1 (empty), return False.

testCases.input: Search: ["a", "ab", "ac"] in trie from test case 4
Search: ["hello", "hell", "he"] in trie from test case 3
Search: ["test", "ten", "tent"] in trie from test case 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Insertion with Collision Resolution in Double-Array Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the insertion operation for DAT, including handling collisions when a child index is already occupied.
ladders.explanation: Insertion in DAT adds a new key by traversing characters and allocating nodes in base and check arrays. The core challenge is collision resolution: when computing t = base[s] + char_code(c), if check[t] is not -1 (empty) and not equal to s (i.e., occupied by another node), we must relocate the conflicting node to a new position. Intuition: This ensures each index in the arrays uniquely represents a parent-child relationship, maintaining the invariant check[t] == parent for valid nodes. Step-by-step: 1. Start at root s=0. 2. For each character c in key: compute t = base[s] + code(c). 3. If check[t] == s, move s = t (node already exists). 4. Else if check[t] == -1, set check[t] = s, base[t] = base[s] (initially, can be adjusted later), and move s = t. 5. Else (collision): let conflict_node = check[t]. Find a new base value for conflict_node such that for all its children, the new indices (new_base + code(child_char)) are free (check[] == -1). Update base[conflict_node] = new_base, move all its children to new indices, update their check values, free old indices. Then retry insertion from current s. 6. After processing all characters, mark s as terminal. Time: O(L + K) where K is the number of children relocated during collisions; space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly to build the full trie. Common mistakes: Infinite loops if collision resolution doesn't terminate; ensure new base is chosen to minimize collisions. Real code pattern: def insert(key): s=0; for c in key: code=char_to_code(c); t=base[s]+code; while check[t] != s and check[t] != -1: relocate(check[t]); t=base[s]+code; if check[t]==-1: check[t]=s; base[t]=base[s]; s=t; terminal.add(s).

examples.input: Insert "bat" into trie with existing "cat" (root s=0, base[0]=1, check[3]=0 for c, base[3]=1)
examples.trace: Existing: for "cat", c (code 2): t=base[0]+2=3, check[3]=0, node 3. Insert "bat": start s=0, b (code 1): t=base[0]+1=2, check[2]==-1, set check[2]=0, base[2]=base[0]=1, s=2. a (code 0): t=base[2]+0=1, check[1]==-1, set check[1]=2, base[1]=base[2]=1, s=1. t (code 19): t=base[1]+19=20, check[20]==-1, set check[20]=1, base[20]=base[1]=1, mark terminal at 20. No collision occurred.

examples.input: Insert "dog" into trie with existing "cat" and "car" (from L0 example 1, after relocation)
examples.trace: From L0 example 1, after inserting "cat" and "car", nodes: root 0, c at 3, a at 1 (after relocation, base[1]=5), children at 5 (a) and 22 (r). Insert "dog": start s=0, d (code 3): t=base[0]+3=4, check[4]==-1, set check[4]=0, base[4]=base[0]=1, s=4. o (code 14): t=base[4]+14=18, check[18]==-1 (free after relocation), set check[18]=4, base[18]=base[4]=1, s=18. g (code 6): t=base[18]+6=24, check[24]==-1, set check[24]=18, base[24]=base[18]=1, mark terminal at 24.

testCases.expected: Nodes created at indices for a and b

Nodes for b and a, no collision

ladders.title: Core Logic — Base and Check Array Representation for Trie Nodes
ladders.desc: Understand how base and check arrays encode parent-child relationships in a trie, enabling compact storage.
ladders.explanation: The core idea of Double-Array Trie is to represent each trie node as an index in two arrays: base and check. For a node with index s, base[s] is an integer offset, and its children are stored at indices t = base[s] + char_code(c) for each character c in the alphabet. The check array verifies the parent-child relationship: check[t] should equal s if t is a valid child of s; otherwise, check[t] is -1 (empty) or another value (indicating a collision). Intuition: This transforms pointer-based trie structures into array-based indexing, reducing memory overhead by eliminating explicit pointers and allowing dense packing. It works because the base value acts as a "base address" for a node's children, and check ensures uniqueness. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize: base[0] = 1 (or another starting offset), check[0] = 0 (root's parent is itself, a sentinel). 2. For insertion/search: given node s and character c, compute child index t = base[s] + code(c). If check[t] == s, then t is a valid child; proceed to t. If check[t] == -1, t is empty; can allocate as child. If check[t] != s and != -1, collision occurs. 3. The invariant: For any valid node t (except root), check[t] equals its parent index. Time: O(1) per transition due to array access; space: O(M) for arrays, where M is size allocated. Connection to L1: This logic is used in insertion to compute child indices and check validity. Common mistakes: Misinterpreting check values; remember check[t] is the parent, not the node itself. Real code pattern: base = [0]*M; check = [-1]*M; base[0]=1; check[0]=0; def get_child(s, c): t=base[s]+ord(c)-ord('a'); return t if check[t]==s else -1.

examples.input: Represent a trie with keys "a" and "b" using base and check arrays
examples.output: base and check arrays showing nodes
examples.trace: Assume alphabet a-z, code a=0, b=1. Initialize: base[0]=1, check[0]=0. Insert "a": s=0, c=a code 0: t=base[0]+0=1, check[1]==-1, set check[1]=0, base[1]=base[0]=1, mark terminal at 1. Insert "b": s=0, c=b code 1: t=base[0]+1=2, check[2]==-1, set check[2]=0, base[2]=base[0]=1, mark terminal at 2. Arrays: base[0]=1, base[1]=1, base[2]=1; check[0]=0, check[1]=0, check[2]=0.

examples.input: Check if "ab" is in trie from example 1
examples.output: False, since no node for b from a
examples.trace: Trie has nodes: root 0, a at 1, b at 2. Search "ab": start s=0, a: t=base[0]+0=1, check[1]==0, valid, s=1. b: t=base[1]+1=2, check[2]==0 (but parent of 2 is 0, not 1), so invalid, return False.

testCases.input: base[0]=1, check[0]=0, compute child index for char 'c' (code 2) from root
base[5]=10, check[10]=5, is index 10 a valid child of 5 for char code 0?
base[3]=7, check[12]=-1, what is the child index for char code 5 from node 3?
base[2]=4, check[9]=2, what is the parent of node 9?

testCases.expected: No, it's occupied by child of 1

testCases.input: base[0]=2, check[2]=0, base[2]=2, check[4]=2, what nodes exist?
testCases.expected: Root 0, child at 2, child of 2 at 4

testCases.input: base[8]=5, check[13]=8, compute char code for child at index 13 from node 8
base[4]=6, check[10]=4, check[11]=-1, which indices are children of 4?
base[7]=1, check[8]=7, base[8]=1, check[9]=8, what is the path for a two-char key?
base[0]=1, check[1]=0, base[1]=1, check[2]=1, is this a valid trie for "ab"? (a code 0, b code 1)

ladders.title: Building Block — Collision Detection and Resolution in Array-Based Structures
ladders.desc: Learn to detect and resolve collisions when multiple nodes map to the same array index in DAT.
ladders.explanation: In Double-Array Trie, collisions occur when two different parent nodes try to place a child at the same array index t, computed as base[s] + char_code(c). This happens because base values might cause overlapping indices. Detection: When inserting, if check[t] is not -1 and not equal to the current parent s, a collision is detected. Resolution involves relocating one of the conflicting nodes to a new base value where all its children can be placed without overlap. Intuition: By adjusting base values, we can "move" a node and its subtree to a different part of the array, freeing up indices for other nodes. This maintains the array's density and avoids infinite loops. Step-by-step: 1. Identify the conflicting node: conflict_node = check[t]. 2. Find a new base value for conflict_node: iterate possible base values (e.g., from 1 upwards) and check if for all current children of conflict_node, the new indices (new_base + char_code(child)) are free (check[] == -1). 3. Update base[conflict_node] = new_base. 4. For each child of conflict_node at old index old_t, compute new index new_t = new_base + char_code(child). Set check[new_t] = conflict_node, base[new_t] = base[old_t] (preserve child's base), and mark old_t as free (set check[old_t] = -1). 5. Retry the insertion from the current node s. Time: O(A * K) in worst case, where A is alphabet size and K is number of children; space: O(1). Connection to L2: This resolves issues in the base-check representation. Common mistakes: Not updating all children during relocation, leading to lost nodes; use a list to track children before moving. Real code pattern: def relocate(node): children=[]; for code in range(alphabet_size): t=base[node]+code; if check[t]==node: children.append((code, t)); new_base=find_free_base(children); base[node]=new_base; for code, old_t in children: new_t=new_base+code; check[new_t]=node; base[new_t]=base[old_t]; check[old_t]=-1.

examples.input: Collision: inserting "dog" after "cat" and "car" (as in L0 example)
examples.trace: From L0: after "cat" and "car", node for 'a' is at index 1 with base[1]=1, children at indices 1 (a) and 18 (r). Insert "dog": at root, d goes to index 4. At node 4, o: t=base[4]+14=18, check[18]==1 (occupied by r), collision with conflict_node=1. Find new base for node 1: try base=5, check children: for a (code 0), new index=5+0=5, check[5]==-1; for r (code 17), new index=5+17=22, check[22]==-1. So new_base=5 works. Update base[1]=5. Move child a: old index 1, new index 5, set check[5]=1, base[5]=base[1]=1, free index 1. Move child r: old index 18, new index 22, set check[22]=1, base[22]=1, free index 18. Now retry o: t=base[4]+14=18, check[18]==-1, proceed.

examples.input: Collision: inserting "ba" after "ab" in empty trie
examples.trace: First insert "ab": root 0, a at index 1, b as child of a at index 2 (since base[1]=1, code b=1: t=2). Insert "ba": start at root, b: t=base[0]+1=2, check[2]==1 (occupied by b from "ab"), collision with conflict_node=1 (parent of index 2). Relocate node 1: find new base, say 3. Children of 1: b at index 2 (code 1). New index for b: 3+1=4, check[4]==-1, so move: set base[1]=3, set check[4]=1, base[4]=base[2]=1, free index 2. Now retry b for "ba": t=base[0]+1=2, check[2]==-1, allocate.

conflict_node=4 with children at indices 7 and 9, find new base starting from 1
testCases.expected: First base where indices new_base+code are free

Collision at index 15 with check[15]=7, current parent s=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Child Index from Parent Using Base and Character Code
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental operation of calculating a child's array index given a parent node and character.
ladders.explanation: In Double-Array Trie, the basic operation is to compute the index t of a child node from a parent node s and a character c. This is done using the formula: t = base[s] + char_code(c), where char_code(c) is an integer representing the character (e.g., 0 for 'a', 1 for 'b', etc.). The base[s] value acts as an offset that determines where the children of node s are stored in the array. Intuition: This transforms the trie traversal from following pointers to simple arithmetic, enabling fast O(1) lookups. It works because we pre-allocate arrays and use base to "reserve" a block of indices for each node's children. Step-by-step: 1. Have a mapping from characters to integer codes, typically 0 to A-1 for alphabet size A. 2. Given parent index s, look up base[s] from the base array. 3. Compute t = base[s] + code(c). 4. Use t to access the check array: if check[t] == s, then t is a valid child; if check[t] == -1, it's empty; otherwise, it indicates a collision or error. Time: O(1) constant time; space: O(1) extra. Connection to L3: This computation is used in collision detection when t is already occupied. Common mistakes: Using wrong char_code mapping (e.g., ASCII values directly, which are too large); normalize to 0-based index. Real code pattern: def char_to_code(c): return ord(c) - ord('a'); def child_index(s, c): return base[s] + char_to_code(c).

examples.input: Parent s=0, base[0]=1, character 'c' (code 2), compute child index
examples.trace: Given: s=0, base[0]=1, c has code 2 (assuming a=0, b=1, c=2). Compute t = base[0] + code('c') = 1 + 2 = 3. So child index is 3.

examples.input: Parent s=5, base[5]=10, character 'z' (code 25), compute child index
examples.trace: s=5, base[5]=10, code('z') = 25 (for a-z alphabet). t = 10 + 25 = 35. Child index is 35.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Character Encoding for Tries
ladders.desc: Understand basic array operations and how to map characters to integers for use in trie structures.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two key concepts: array indexing and character encoding. In programming, arrays are contiguous memory locations accessed by indices (e.g., arr[i] for element at index i). For tries, we use arrays to store node information efficiently. Character encoding involves converting characters (like letters) to integer codes, typically starting from 0 for 'a', 1 for 'b', etc., to use as offsets in array calculations. Intuition: By mapping characters to small integers, we can compute positions in an array predictably, which is essential for the Double-Array Trie where we need to find child indices quickly. Step-by-step: 1. Array indexing: If we have an array of size N, indices range from 0 to N-1. Accessing arr[k] gives the value at position k. 2. Character encoding: For a lowercase English alphabet, define code(c) = ord(c) - ord('a'), where ord() gets the ASCII value (e.g., ord('a')=97, so code('a')=0, code('b')=1, ..., code('z')=25). This creates a 0-based index. 3. Combine: To find where to store a child for character c from a parent node, we use an offset (base value) plus code(c) to get an array index. Time: O(1) for both operations; space: O(1). Connection to L4: This enables the computation of child indices. Common mistakes: Forgetting to subtract ord('a') leading to large indices; ensure codes fit within array bounds. Real code pattern: arr = [0] * 100; index = 5; arr[index] = 10; code = ord('c') - ord('a'); print(code) # outputs 2.

examples.trace: Use formula code(c) = ord(c) - ord('a'). ord('a')=97, so code('a')=97-97=0. ord('b')=98, code('b')=98-97=1. ord('c')=99, code('c')=99-97=2. Result: [0, 1, 2].

examples.input: Given array arr = [10, 20, 30], access index 1 and compute index for character 'd'
examples.trace: Array arr has indices 0,1,2. arr[1] is 20. For character 'd', ord('d')=100, code('d')=100 - ord('a')=100-97=3. So index for 'd' in computations would be 3.
